title: The last fluent speaker
summary: On the Latvian coast, an 88-year-old woman is the last fluent speaker of Livonian — and she has decided what her daily life should look like in the language that nobody else fully understands.
body:
Grizelda Kristiņa lives in a fishing village on the Latvian coast. She is eighty-eight. She is the last person who learned Livonian as a child, at home, before she learned any other language. There are roughly two hundred people who can hold a short conversation in Livonian. There is no one left who dreams in it except her.

Linguists visit. They have been visiting for forty years. Some come from Helsinki, some from Tartu, one from Tokyo. They record her telling stories her grandmother told her. They write down small words for tools that no longer exist — a hook for a net that nobody fishes with any more, the verb for salting a fish in a wooden barrel. Grizelda is patient with them, although she has said, more than once, that she is tired of being a museum.

What the visitors do not always see is the rest of her day. Grizelda speaks Livonian when she is alone. She reads the newspaper in Latvian, then translates the headlines into Livonian aloud, for nobody. She cooks while naming the ingredients in Livonian. She has a notebook, half-filled, in which she writes the Livonian word for a thing she has thought of that day — 'the sound a kettle makes before it boils', 'the colour of the sea in November'. She has decided that the language will continue to live inside her ordinary life, whether or not anyone is recording.

A young man from a nearby town visits once a fortnight. His grandmother had been Livonian; he is trying to learn the language as an adult. Grizelda is gentle with him. She corrects only the mistakes that matter and lets the small ones pass. She says the language will not survive in the form she learned it, and she has accepted that. What she hopes is that it will survive at all — perhaps spoken slowly, perhaps with borrowed words from Latvian, perhaps mostly written.

A visitor asked her, last summer, whether she felt lonely as the last fluent speaker. Grizelda thought for a while. She said the loneliness was not the kind people imagined. She did not miss being understood. She missed, sometimes, being misunderstood — the small daily moments when someone replies a little wrong and you laugh. That, she said, is the life of a language. Without it, the language is already in the archive, although she is still here.

annotations.meaningVi: tiếng Livonia (ngôn ngữ Finno-Ugric ven biển Latvia)
duy trì một cuộc trò chuyện ngắn
mệt vì bị xem như bảo tàng (sống mà bị quan sát)
từ vay mượn (từ ngôn ngữ khác)
đã vào kho lưu trữ (chỉ còn là tài liệu)

grammarNotes.title: Relative clauses with 'who/which/that'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Mệnh đề quan hệ (relative clause) cung cấp thêm thông tin về danh từ. Trong bài: 'She is the last person who learned Livonian as a child' — 'who learned…' xác định 'the last person'. 'A hook for a net that nobody fishes with any more' — 'that nobody fishes with' xác định 'a net'. 'A young man from a nearby town visits once a fortnight. His grandmother had been Livonian' — câu sau có thể viết gọn thành 'A young man whose grandmother had been Livonian visits once a fortnight'. Tại B1, ta dùng 'who' cho người, 'that/which' cho vật, 'whose' chỉ sở hữu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Relative clauses add information about a noun. In the passage: 'She is the last person who learned Livonian as a child' — 'who learned…' identifies 'the last person'. 'A hook for a net that nobody fishes with any more' — 'that nobody fishes with' identifies 'a net'. A B1 rewrite of the next sentences would be 'A young man whose grandmother had been Livonian visits once a fortnight.' Use 'who' for people, 'that/which' for things, 'whose' for possession.

translationVi:
Grizelda Kristiņa sống trong một làng chài trên bờ biển Latvia. Bà tám mươi tám tuổi. Bà là người cuối cùng học tiếng Livonia khi còn nhỏ, ở nhà, trước khi học bất kỳ ngôn ngữ nào khác. Có khoảng hai trăm người có thể duy trì một cuộc trò chuyện ngắn bằng tiếng Livonia. Không ai còn nằm mơ bằng nó trừ bà.

Các nhà ngôn ngữ học đến thăm. Họ đã đến trong bốn mươi năm. Vài người từ Helsinki, vài người từ Tartu, một người từ Tokyo. Họ ghi âm bà kể những câu chuyện bà ngoại đã kể cho bà. Họ viết ra những từ nhỏ chỉ những công cụ không còn tồn tại — cái móc cho một loại lưới không ai đánh nữa, động từ chỉ việc muối cá trong thùng gỗ. Grizelda kiên nhẫn với họ, dù bà đã nói nhiều lần rằng bà mệt vì bị xem như bảo tàng.

Cái khách thăm không phải lúc nào cũng thấy là phần còn lại trong ngày của bà. Grizelda nói tiếng Livonia khi ở một mình. Bà đọc báo bằng tiếng Latvia, rồi dịch các tiêu đề sang Livonia thành tiếng, cho không ai cả. Bà nấu ăn và gọi tên các nguyên liệu bằng Livonia. Bà có một cuốn sổ viết nửa, ghi từ Livonia chỉ một thứ bà nghĩ ra trong ngày — 'âm thanh ấm nước phát ra trước khi sôi', 'màu của biển vào tháng Mười Một'. Bà đã quyết định ngôn ngữ sẽ tiếp tục sống trong cuộc sống thường nhật của bà, dù có ai ghi lại hay không.

Một chàng trai từ một thị trấn gần đó đến thăm hai tuần một lần. Bà ngoại của anh đã là người Livonia; anh đang cố học ngôn ngữ này khi trưởng thành. Grizelda dịu dàng với anh. Bà chỉ sửa các lỗi quan trọng và bỏ qua lỗi nhỏ. Bà nói ngôn ngữ sẽ không sống sót theo hình thức bà đã học, và bà chấp nhận. Cái bà mong là nó còn sống — có thể nói chậm, có thể có từ vay từ tiếng Latvia, có thể chủ yếu được viết.

Một khách hỏi bà, hè năm ngoái, liệu bà có thấy cô đơn khi là người cuối cùng thông thạo. Grizelda nghĩ một lát. Bà nói nỗi cô đơn không phải kiểu người ta tưởng. Bà không nhớ việc được hiểu. Bà nhớ, đôi khi, việc bị hiểu sai — những khoảnh khắc nhỏ trong ngày khi ai đó đáp lại hơi sai và ta cười. Đó, bà nói, là cuộc sống của một ngôn ngữ. Không có nó, ngôn ngữ đã ở trong kho lưu trữ, dù bà vẫn còn đây.

questions.prompt: Where does Grizelda live and how old is she?
options: Fishing village on the Latvian coast; 88

questions.prompt: How many people can hold a short conversation in Livonian?

options: Stories from her grandmother, and small words for tools that no longer exist

questions.prompt: How does Grizelda describe the experience of being visited so much?
options: She is tired of being a museum

questions.prompt: What does she do when she is alone?
options: Speaks Livonian — translates headlines, names ingredients, writes new words in a notebook

questions.prompt: Give two examples of phrases in her notebook.
options: 'The sound a kettle makes before it boils' and 'the colour of the sea in November'

questions.prompt: Who is the young man and how often does he visit?
options: His grandmother had been Livonian; he is learning as an adult; once a fortnight
He is a linguist from Tokyo, once a year

questions.prompt: What does Grizelda hope the language will be?
options: Survive at all — perhaps spoken slowly, with borrowed words, perhaps mostly written

questions.prompt: What kind of loneliness does she describe?
options: Not missing being understood, but missing being misunderstood — the small replies that go a bit wrong

questions.prompt: What is her test for whether a language is alive?
options: Daily moments of being misunderstood — without them, the language is already in the archive

questions.prompt: Which is NOT in the passage?
questions.explanation: She does not teach — linguists visit her.

options: A last fluent speaker insists her language stay alive in ordinary daily moments, not only in archives
Latvian and Livonian are the same

cloze.template:
Grizelda Kristiņa lives in a fishing village on the Latvian coast. She is eighty-eight. She is the last person who learned Livonian as a {{b1}}, at home, before she learned any other language. There are roughly two hundred people who can hold a short {{b2}} in Livonian. There is no one left who dreams in it except her.

Linguists visit. They have been visiting for forty years. Some come from Helsinki, some from Tartu, one from {{b3}}. They record her telling stories her grandmother told her. They write down small words for tools that no longer exist — a hook for a net that nobody fishes with any more, the verb for salting a fish in a wooden {{b4}}. Grizelda is patient with them, although she has said, more than once, that she is tired of being a {{b5}}.

What the visitors do not always see is the rest of her day. Grizelda speaks Livonian when she is alone. She reads the newspaper in Latvian, then translates the headlines into Livonian {{b6}}, for nobody. She cooks while naming the ingredients in Livonian. She has a notebook, half-filled, in which she writes the Livonian word for a thing she has thought of that day — 'the sound a kettle makes before it boils', 'the colour of the sea in November'. She has decided that the language will continue to live inside her {{b7}} life, whether or not anyone is recording.

A young man from a nearby town visits once a fortnight. His grandmother had been Livonian; he is trying to learn the language as an adult. Grizelda is gentle with him. She corrects only the mistakes that matter and lets the small ones {{b8}}. She says the language will not survive in the form she learned it, and she has accepted that.

A visitor asked her, last summer, whether she felt lonely as the last fluent speaker. Grizelda thought for a while. She said the loneliness was not the kind people imagined. She did not miss being understood. She missed, sometimes, being {{b9}} — the small daily moments when someone replies a little wrong and you laugh. That, she said, is the life of a language. Without it, the language is already in the {{b10}}, although she is still here.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Grizelda's test for whether a language is alive is small daily misunderstandings, not the number of speakers or the size of the archive. Apply her test to something you care about — a craft, a family recipe, a song tradition. Is it alive by her standard? Why or why not?
